learning_outcomes.description: demonstrate an ability to think sociologically about inequality, including what social inequality is, why it occurs, what its impact is, and possible responses
critically reflect on your own experiences and positionality in relation to inequality and privilege
develop a sense of agency and engage with solution focused social science
develop communication skills to advocate for social justice initiatives

assessments.due_date:
Exam 1a - 15% (Week 4: during class time)

Exam 1b - 20% (Week 8: during class time)

Exam 1c - 25% (Week 11: during class time)

The exams are held during the student's scheduled tutorial class. Classes occur on different days and at different times for different students.

The exam is scheduled for 60 minutes. This includes any reading and planning. To accommodate students with AEAs or who need additional reading or writing time, an additional 30 minutes will be provided, making a total of 90 minutes for those who need it. Please see Student Support and Welfare Services about an AEA if you require other exam adjustments.
assessments.task_description:
Assessment 1 is a series of exams that you will engage in during class time 3 times throughout the semester:

* The first exam (1a) occurs in Week 4 of semester, is worth 15%

* The second exam (1b) occurs in Week 8 of semester and is worth 20%

* The third exam (1c) occurs in Week 11 of semester and is worth 25%

The exam is held during tutorial time and is scheduled for 60 minutes. The format is that you will read through a case study and respond to a question. Your hand-written response will be a maximum of 500 words. The case study and question will be based on the work you have done in the modules and the in-class exercises and discussions that have occurred prior to the assessment week.

Please see Blackboard and the Course Study Guide for further details about the task and the marking criteria. 

Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI): This assessment task will be completed in-person. The use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Exams will be undertaken and submitted during class time.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

assessments.due_date:
30/10/2026 2:00 pm

Please see the Study Guide and Blackboard for details and assistance with the task, including the marking rubric and a suggested work plan.
assessments.task_description:
In this assignment, you will create a 3-minute video pitch in response to a scenario, to articulate the importance of responding to a particular area of social inequality. Drawing on concepts learned in the course, and your own research, you will identify a compelling argument for addressing systemic disparities and injustices in a particular area, propose a path of action and present your pitch in a concise and persuasive manner.

Please see Blackboard for details of the task, including the scenario, submission requirements, and uploading instructions, as well as the marking criteria that will be used to assess your work.

AI Use: This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which AI may provide limited support and guidance. 

A failure to reference AI use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct Policy. Students may be required to complete an AI usage declaration and submit it with their assessment. Additional resources are available through UQ AI Student Hub.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You are required to submit your video using the ECHO360 platform. No other submission formats will be accepted. Please see Blackboard for details of this process.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

learning_activities.topic:
Introduction: Welcome & What is Social Inequality?

Welcome to week 1 of SOCY1070 which serves as an introduction to the course.

  1. There is an online Learning Module for Week 1 to help you get oriented to the course and to provide you with links to some helpful resources and support. You are expected to come to class having read the Course Profile and the Course Study Guide as well as this Learning Module. 
  2. Building on what you have learned from the Study Guide for the course, in this week’s Tutorial we look at how learning will happen in the course, what you need to do to pass the course, and the resources that will assist you. You will be invited to reflect on your personal connections to the topic of inequality through a critical incident timeline. We will then introduce two key theories that underpin this course.

learning_activities.topic:
How do we know if/where social inequality exists?

Measurement is one means by which we can recognise the existence of inequality, and understand whether it is changing – for better or worse. However, this is not a neutral, straightforward process:

  1. In this week’s Learning Module, we examine the measurement of inequality, via a series of video lectures. Please read the online module prior to your tutorial.
  2. Our in-class Tutorial experience provides opportunities to ground these concepts and clarify your understanding.
learning_activities.topic_title: How do we know if/where social inequality exists?

learning_activities.topic:
Why does social inequality matter? (Online only)

Welcome to Week 3 of SOCY1070 where we look more closely at why social inequality matters, and why, as social scientists we might be interested in it.

  1. Our online Learning Module introduces this topic with interviews, videos and readings. To complement this and assist you particularly with Assessment 2, we also have a Learning Module from the library which helps you with search tips and an orientation to research. As there is a public holiday in Brisbane, there are no face-to-face Tutorials in this course this week, only the online modules for you to complete. Please reach out to your tutors should you need clarity about any of the module material.

learning_activities.topic:
What causes social inequality?

Our focus this week is on the causes of social inequality.

  1. Through the online Learning Module, we begin with the idea of othering, and essentialising the other. We also distinguish between social categorisation and social devaluation and look at how judgement cycles are formed at the individual and social levels and explore the important topic of “unconscious bias”. Please read the online module prior to your tutorial.
  2. This week our assessment commences. The Tutorial time will be used to complete the first in our series of in-class exams. You can see further details about Assessment 1 in the course Study Guide. On Blackboard there are additional resources to help you prepare for this task and know what to expect.

On Blackboard there are additional resources to help you prepare for this task and know what to expect.

learning_activities.topic:
Is social inequality inevitable?

  1. In this week’s online Learning Module we ask, “Is social inequality inevitable?” by thinking about the history of inequality and how it has manifested over time. We will also ask the question, “So, are things getting better or worse?” and consider whether we are getting closer to equality for all. You might be surprised at the answer. This week we mainly rely on videos for our content so you might want to prepare the popcorn! Please engage with the online module prior to your tutorial.
  2. This week’s Tutorial helps pull together the ideas from the last few weeks: why social devaluation matters, why it occurs, and whether it is inevitable.

This week’s Tutorial helps pull together the ideas from the last few weeks: why social devaluation matters, why it occurs, and whether it is inevitable.

learning_activities.topic:
Inequality and the impact of colonisation

Last week we took a broad historical lens to the idea of social inequality. This week we narrow our focus to the specific topic of colonialisation and the impact of colonisation on First nations people. We do this by engaging with the excellent online module “History lives in us”.

  1. The online Learning Module examines how events and policies in the past have shaped the identities and experiences of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples today. The module looks at Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander activism; the ‘rights’ movement and campaigns waged by key organisations in the lead up to the establishment of the Commonwealth Department of Aboriginal Affairs in 1972. Please read the online module prior to your tutorial.
  2. This week’s Tutorial invites further engagement with the module and examines in what ways this history is still powerfully with us and the consequences for first nations people globally and in Australia.

learning_activities.topic:
Theories of inequality - Intersectionality and Privilege

Often, sociology courses and texts on inequality provide a weekly run-down on different axes of inequality: gender in Week 1, race in Week 2, class in Week 3, and so on. In this course, we have deliberately chosen not to do that, but to instead foreground intersectional understandings of inequality.

  1. This week's Learning Module focuses on this idea by introducing the topics of positionality, privilege, intersectionality and compound disadvantage. Please read the online module prior to your tutorial.
  2. We will continue this discussion in our Tutorial where we will invite you to think about your own connections to privilege and intersectionality with a series of in-class exercises. As self-identified black, lesbian, feminist, mother, poet, warrior, and cancer-survivor, Audre Lorde (1984) says, “There is no such thing as a single-issue struggle because we do not live single-issue lives.”

learning_activities.topic:
Social inequality over time

Week 8 focuses on the evolution of inequality over time, examining individual fluctuations and life course perspectives.

  1. In Week 8's online Learning Module, Associate Professor Paco Perales's recorded lecture poses the question: does being born poor mean staying poor? We will also delve into intergenerational transmission of advantage/disadvantage, exploring broader societal impacts. Transitioning from micro to macro analysis, we will scrutinise the role of social institutions in shaping equal or unequal societies. Please read the online module prior to your tutorial.
  2. In Week 8's Tutorial you will undertake the second of your in-class exams. This task is worth 20% of your grade. You can see further details about the assessment in the course Study Guide.

learning_activities.topic:
Theories of inequality - Power/Knowledge

For those of you who have previously studied sociology, you were likely introduced to some of key thinkers in sociological theory: Marx, Weber, Durkheim, for example.In the course we continue to reference these important thinkers, but we also want to bring in a different theoretical perspective by thinking about the work of Michel Foucault.

  1. This week's Learning Module introduces Foucault's ideas of power/knowledge and discipline, showing how these have shaped our thinking in new and exciting ways and given us new understandings of the dynamics of inequality. Please read the online module prior to your tutorial.
  2. In this week’s Tutorial we will apply Foucault’s thinking about power/knowledge and use a jigsaw exercise to help us work through some of his key ideas.

learning_activities.topic:
Responding to social Inequality – Solution focused social science (Online only)

So far we have spent the semester exploring social inequality – what it is, why it exists, how it can be measured, and its various often devastating impacts. For the last few weeks of semester we are looking at how we might respond, which links to the idea of “Solution focused social science”.

  1. Our online Learning Module this week introduces this idea, suggesting that the role of social science is not simply to study phenomena, but to provide solutions towards resolving modern social challenges. 
  2. As Monday of this week is a public holiday in Brisbane there are no Tutorials scheduled. However, we will be monitoring online communications should you have any queries about the online materials.

Our online Learning Module this week introduces this idea, suggesting that the role of social science is not simply to study phenomena, but to provide solutions towards resolving modern social challenges. learning_activities.topic:
What strategies can we use for social transformation?

Throughout this course we have highlighted the central concepts of agency and structure. We have spent the last section of the course looking at how structure operates.

  1. In this week's online Learning Module we flip to thinking about agency – first of all, how we exercise individual agency, as well as the limits of our individual agency. Then we begin thinking about collective agency, how we can work deliberately to create change in social structures. Please engage with the online module prior to your tutorial.
  2. In our Tutorial we will undertake the third of our in-class exams during class-time .

learning_activities.topic:
How do I plan for future impact?

  1. Join us for our final Learning Module, where we focus on employability and help you use what you have learned in this course, in your thinking about future career options.
  2. In our final Tutorial for the semester we work through the ideas of collective agency for change, and provide opportunity for any final conversations about Assessment 2.

learning_activities.topic:
Assessment 2

There is no Learning Module or Tutorial during week 13. You are encouraged to use this time prepare your final assessment item for the course (Assessment 2 - due Friday 2pm this week). Your tutors will be available for consultation. They will advise you of the arrangements for this.
